payload.text:
Lav et simpelt, farverigt læringsspil til børn, hvor spilleren skal sortere forskellige objekter (f.eks. bolde, æbler, stjerner og terninger) ned i fire spande.

Hver spand har en unik farve og et ikon, så det er tydeligt, hvor tingene hører til.

Når spilleren trækker et objekt over i den korrekte spand, skal der automatisk opdateres en søjlediagram-graf ude i højre side af skærmen. For hver genstand, der lægges i en spand, skal den tilhørende søjle i grafen stige med én enhed.

Grafen skal vise:
– Én søjle pr. spand
– Højde = antal sorterede genstande
– Tallene skal være lette at aflæse for børn

Når alle objekterne er sorteret, skal spilleren få en opgave:
“Hvilken spand har flest ting?” eller “Hvor mange æbler blev der samlet i alt?”

Spillet skal derfor både træne sortering, tælleforståelse og læsning af grafer.

Grafen skal opdatere live i realtid undervejs, så spilleren ser den direkte forbindelse mellem handling (sortering) og repræsentation (graf).

**Considering the Core Goal**

I'm focusing now on understanding the user's ultimate objective: a simple, vibrant, Danish educational game for kids. The key mechanics are taking shape, and I'm honing in on the initial features. My attention is drawn to the core interaction and intended learning outcomes.


**Defining the Components**

I've outlined the core components: the draggable items and the target buckets. Each item will have an ID, type, color, and icon for visual clarity. The buckets will similarly have identifiers. My state management will encompass the items, buckets, game state, and details for the quiz. I'm prioritizing simplicity. I'm beginning to solidify the technical foundation with React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS.


**Incorporating Feedback and Design**

I'm now integrating feedback mechanisms into the quiz, defining correct/incorrect responses. Drag-and-drop mechanics will utilize the HTML5 API to ensure simplicity and adherence to the prompt. My design emphasizes a split-screen layout, bright colors, and large, rounded fonts. Animations will provide visual feedback on correct item placement.


**Adding the Gemini Integration**

Now I'm focusing on the integration of `@google/genai` for quiz generation. I'm crafting the prompt to ensure relevant, age-appropriate questions in Danish. I've designed the JSON structure for input data and outlined expected answer formatting. The goal is to make the experience smooth and self-correcting.


**Tackling D&D and Charts**

I'm now prioritizing the drag-and-drop and chart functionality. I'm focusing on managing React re-renders with HTML5's D&D API, ensuring data transfer between drag and drop events. For charts, I'm mapping my `buckets` state to the format required by Recharts, defining the data objects. Lastly, I'm making sure my Gemini API integration handles missing API keys gracefully. I'm focusing on the integration for the "End Game" phase.


**Refining the UI Components**

I'm now iterating on the visual aspects, focusing on a "pool" of items at the top for responsive layouts, instead of absolute positioning. I'm also drafting the core files: `App.tsx` and `LiveChart.tsx`. `App.tsx` will initialize the `items` with a random count, handle the drop, and trigger the quiz fetch. `LiveChart.tsx` will utilize a Recharts `<BarChart>` with custom `Bar` components matching the bucket colors. I'm focusing on language as well, ensuring everything is in Danish and has a "kid-friendly" design.

entries.description: App metadata definition
Root HTML file with Tailwind CSS and viewport settings
React entry point
TypeScript definitions for the application
Global constants and initial data configurations
Service to interact with Google Gemini API for quiz generation
Component for the drop target (Bucket)
Component for the draggable objects
Recharts implementation for live data visualization
Main application logic

payload.text: Er det muligt at få ikoner der ligner mere? Det er meget outline agtigt, og ikke særlig venligt til børn i indskolingen. Ideen fungerer super godt ellers

**Refining Iconography Choices**

I'm thinking about making the app's icons more appealing to children. I want to move away from the current outline style and use more solid, colorful visuals. The goal is to design visuals that are very simple, and easily understood by students in "indskoling." I'm considering using a more vibrant or emoji-like style.


**Prioritizing Emoji Implementation**

Okay, I've hit a snag with external dependencies. Emojis seem like the cleanest win here. I can replace the Lucide icons directly with emojis in `BucketData` and the components. I'll prioritize that approach first, since it is easy to implement. I may need to create custom SVGs if emojis fail to meet the "look more like" requirement. I'm excited to see how this approach works.


**Crafting SVG Alternatives**

I'm now prioritizing the creation of custom, filled SVG components for the icons. The goal is consistent, child-friendly visuals across platforms. I'm planning to define `AppleIcon`, `BallIcon`, `StarIcon`, and `DiceIcon` in `components/Icons.tsx`. I'll also modify `types.ts` to accommodate the new icon components, and update the existing components to use the custom icons. I will try to keep the same API.


**Visualizing Color Palettes**

I'm now focusing on the visual design of the icons themselves. I've decided to give them intrinsic colors for a more realistic look, ignoring the `fill` prop passed from `bucketConfig`. Specifically, the apple will be red with a green leaf, the ball will take the bucket color, the star will be yellow, and the die will be purple with white dots. This approach should yield a consistent, intuitive visual language.


**Implementing Icon SVGs**

I'm now implementing the SVG icons, like `AppleIcon`, `BallIcon`, `StarIcon`, and `DiceIcon`, based on the color scheme from `constants.tsx`. I will be updating `types.ts` to accommodate `React.FC<any>` icon components. The new icons will be defined in `components/Icons.tsx`, and I'll modify existing components like `Bucket.tsx` and `DraggableItem.tsx` to use the new icons. I'm focusing on making sure they handle styling consistently.


**Detailing New Components**

I'm creating a new file, `components/GameIcons.tsx`, for full-color, hardcoded icons. I'm starting with `AppleIcon` (red body, green leaf), `BallIcon` (simple sphere with a highlight), and `StarIcon` (rounded corners). These should bypass the `color` override from `DraggableItem`, for a more realistic look. My plan is to make the visuals friendly and consistent.


**Adjusting Item Visuals**

I'm now detailing the individual item visuals and the plan to integrate them. The goal is to enforce a consistent color scheme derived from the bucket colors for a more realistic look. For instance, the apple will be red, the ball will take the bucket color, the star will be yellow, and the die will be purple, all with a "filled" and "soft" visual style. I'll modify `types.ts` to use `React.FC<React.SVGProps<SVGSVGElement>>` for the `icon` type.


**Evaluating Item Styling**

I'm now making adjustments to how the icons are styled, focusing on the `DraggableItem` component. If the icons are self-colored, applying `text-gray-700` can cause issues. I'm considering removing the `style={{ color: bucketConfig.fill }}` as well. I'm torn between hardcoding colors for a better look and maintaining flexibility. The current plan is to use a `fill` prop but default to a good look, and define separate "Game Icons" for specific visuals.


**Designing Custom Icon SVGs**

I'm now in the midst of implementing the custom SVG icons, specifically the apple, ball, star, and dice. The plan involves creating components with the same `size` and `className` props as `lucide-react` icons for seamless replacement. For the apple, I will use a red body with a green leaf. The ball will be blue with a highlight. I'm imagining a yellow star, and a purple dice with white dots, aiming for an intuitive visual style.


**Simplifying Icon Paths**

I'm now revising my SVG paths for the custom icons to streamline them and achieve a slightly cartoonish, child-friendly aesthetic. I'm focusing on simplicity for better inline rendering. I'll remove the strict color classes and implement changes in `types.ts`, `constants.tsx`, `components/GameIcons.tsx`, and `components/Bucket.tsx`.

To make the icons more child-friendly, solid, and colorful ("filled" instead of outlines), I will create a new set of custom SVG components (`components/GameIcons.tsx`) that represent the Apple, Ball, Star, and Dice with a playful, cartoon-like design.

Here are the changes:

1.  **Create `components/GameIcons.tsx`**: Contains custom multi-colored SVG icons.
2.  **Update `types.ts`**: Relax the `icon` type definition to allow these new components.
3.  **Update `constants.tsx`**: Use the new icons.
4.  **Update `components/DraggableItem.tsx`**: Remove the CSS color override so the icons' natural colors show.
5.  **Update `components/Bucket.tsx`**: Adjust rendering to ensure icons look good on the buckets.

entries.description: Update icon type to allow custom components
New file with colorful custom SVG icons
Switch to using custom GameIcons
Remove color tinting to show natural icon colors
Adjust bucket icon rendering

payload.text: Kan vi få flere versioner? Så der er flere forskellige ting der skal sorteres? Bare så man ikke skal sortere de samme ting igen og igen

**Examining Item Variations**

I'm currently focused on the need for more diverse sortable items. The user's feedback highlights the need for a wider range of versions beyond the standard Apple, Ball, Star, and Dice. I'm prioritizing adding more variety to maintain engagement and provide a richer sorting experience.


**Introducing Thematic Levels**

I've decided to incorporate themes, or levels, to increase the variety of items. Instead of a single hardcoded bucket set, I will now randomly select a set at the start or reset. I've brainstormed several themes: shapes/toys, animals, vehicles, nature, and food. The design will remain simple, colorful, and filled. This means I'll need to create new SVGs for the different themes. Each icon should have a 100x100 viewBox.


**Defining New Item Enums**

I'm now deep in the code, specifically `types.ts` and `constants.tsx`. I'm expanding the `ItemType` enum to include the new items, or maybe using string literals. I'm also renaming `INITIAL_BUCKETS` to `GAME_THEMES`. This will now be an array of Theme objects with the themename and their BucketConfigs. I'm also preparing to make the `resetGame` function work with the themes. I've started drafting the icons themselves in `components/GameIcons.tsx`!


**Incorporating New Icons**

I'm currently working to expand the `ItemType` enum to include `CAT`, `DOG`, `FISH`, `BIRD`, `CAR`, `PLANE`, `BOAT`, and `ROCKET`. I'm also updating `constants.tsx` to include new `THEMES` array, and I've moved the existing bucket configuration into it. The reset game logic is being updated to select a new random theme. Finally, I'm thinking of a visual indicator for the theme in the header.


**Optimizing Icon Creation**

I'm now focusing on ensuring the new SVG icons are simple, avoiding overly complex shapes to minimize errors. I'm using circles, rectangles, and paths for items like the cat (circle head, triangle ears), fish (oval body, triangle tail), car (rect body, circle wheels), and plane (cross shape). I am updating `components/GameIcons.tsx`, `types.ts`, `constants.tsx`, and `App.tsx` accordingly. Finally, I'm making sure to prevent the same theme from being selected twice in a row when resetting the game. Also, I'm ensuring the icon colors align with the bucket colors, i.e. Fish = Blue bucket.

Selvfølgelig! Det er en rigtig god idé at variere indholdet for at holde børnenes interesse fanget.

Jeg udvider spillet med et "Tema-system". Hver gang man starter et nyt spil, vælger appen tilfældigt mellem tre forskellige verdener:
1.  **Klassisk:** (De nuværende: Æbler, Bolde, Stjerner, Terninger)
2.  **Dyr:** (Kat, Hund, Fisk, Fugl)
3.  **Sommer:** (Sol, Blomst, Træ, Is)

Her er ændringerne:

1.  **Nye Ikoner:** Jeg tilføjer 8 nye håndtegnede, farverige SVG-ikoner.
2.  **Nye Typer:** Opdaterer TypeScript-definitionerne.
3.  **Tema-konfiguration:** Omdanner `INITIAL_BUCKETS` til en liste af temaer.
4.  **Spillogik:** Opdaterer `App.tsx` til at vælge et tilfældigt tema ved start.

entries.description: Adding new colorful icons for Animals and Summer themes
Expanded ItemType enum to include new themes
Defined themes and their bucket configurations
Update game reset logic to pick random themes

payload.text: Kan du ikke lave endnu flere ting? Mindst 32 forskellige ting der skal sorteres

**Planning Game Expansion**

I'm currently focused on game expansion planning. The goal is at least 32 sortable items, a substantial increase from the current 12. I'm starting by brainstorming additional themes beyond the existing ones, as this is the most critical step. I'm exploring different content types to see what will fit well.


**Exploring New Game Themes**

I've been iterating on new game themes. My focus is on expanding to at least eight, building toward the 32-item target. I've solidified "Vehicles" and "Space" themes, and I'm considering "Food," "Weather," "Sports," "School," and "Music." I'm also re-evaluating the "Summer" theme, which is now "Nature/Summer." I will be solidifying the last two themes today.


**Implementing Expansion Items**

I'm now fully immersed in the implementation phase. I've finalized the new themes: Food, Sports, and School, each with four items to reach the target of 32 total items. My next steps are icon creation in `GameIcons.tsx`, updating the `ItemType` enum, and incorporating these new themes into the `THEMES` constant. It's time to refine the game's visuals!


**Expanding Game Item Set**

I've been working on a new set of items. I'm building on Space, Food, Sports, and School themes. I've chosen items like a Rocket, Pizza, Soccer Ball, and Book, aiming for diversity and visual appeal. I've updated the `GameIcons.tsx` component to include these, as well as the `types.ts` and `constants.tsx` files. My immediate focus is fine-tuning the visuals for all 32 items.


**Refining Item Icons**

I'm currently working to refine the icons for all items. I'm focusing on "Space," "Food," "Sports," "School," and "Vehicles." I've selected items like a rocket, pizza, soccer ball, backpack, and train. I'm preparing to update `types.ts`, `GameIcons.tsx`, and `constants.tsx` to accommodate the 32 items. Now I will write the XML changes.


**Crafting Item Visuals**

I'm now diving into the SVG icon creation. My goal is to keep them simple yet visually appealing, emphasizing color and fill. I've begun crafting icons for "Vehicles", "Space", "Food", "Sports", and "School". I've planned out basic SVG components like body, wheels, windows, etc. for each item, and I'm iterating on their design. I'm focusing on "CarIcon", "RocketIcon", "PizzaIcon", "SoccerIcon", and "BookIcon" as initial priorities.

Det er en super idé! Mere variation gør spillet meget sjovere at vende tilbage til.

Jeg udvider spillet markant med **5 nye temaer**, så der nu er i alt **8 temaer** med samlet **32 forskellige ting** at sortere.

De nye temaer er:
1.  **Køretøjer:** (Bil, Båd, Fly, Tog)
2.  **Rummet:** (Raket, Planet, Måne, UFO)
3.  **Mad:** (Pizza, Burger, Donut, Pomfritter)
4.  **Sport:** (Fodbold, Basketbold, Tennisbold, Baseball)
5.  **Skole:** (Bog, Blyant, Rygsæk, Lineal)

Her er opdateringerne:

entries.description: Expanding ItemType enum to include 5 new themes (Total 32 items)
Adding 20 new SVG icons for the new themes
Configuring 8 unique themes with distinct colors and icons

payload.text: Kan vi gøre så kasserne er tomme indtil man putter noget i dem? Sådan at eleverne bedre får en forståelse for at kategorier er noget man selv skal lave når man sorterer data

**Defining Initial States**

I'm currently focused on the initial state of the sorting buckets. My analysis reveals that the core request is to have them appear empty. This setup is strategically educational. I'm exploring how this blank canvas can help students better grasp the abstract nature of categories, fostering a deeper understanding of classification. I'm considering the pedagogical implications.


**Refining Bucket Visualization**

I've been iterating on the visual representation of the buckets. The goal is "empty until filled." I've considered several approaches, like complete hiding (too difficult), greyed-out placeholders, and generic "Empty Bin" icons that transform. Currently, I'm leaning toward a hybrid: buckets will be visible but neutral initially. Their specific category attributes (color, icon, label) will activate upon receiving the first item.


**Analyzing Activation Logic**

I'm now deep-diving into the activation logic for the sorting buckets. Initially, I was playing with making the buckets generic "New Category" slots. However, I've reframed it as buckets that start "locked" or "neutral" with a grey background and a silhouette icon. The core of this hinges on the visual transition and how the category attributes (color, icon) activate upon the first item drop. I'm focusing on the user experience.


**Optimizing Dragging Feedback**

I'm now focusing on how the item-dragging experience should unfold. Initially, I considered providing hints about bucket placement, but I've rejected this in favor of a trial-and-error approach. However, I've re-evaluated this and am considering gray outlines with a visible icon silhouette. These empty wireframes would then burst into color upon the first item drop, reinforcing the idea of "empty until filled."


**Refining Slot Dynamics**

I'm now zeroing in on the interaction of the slots. The "categories are something you make" concept is now leading me to favor unassigned slots that become "Apple Bucket" or "Car Bucket" when the first item lands. I'm exploring the implications of dynamic assignment versus simpler pre-assigned "ghost" buckets that start blank but reveal their category. While dynamic assignment is excellent conceptually, I'm considering the complexity and the age of the users. I'm prioritizing user experience and code simplicity.


**Implementing Visual Cues**

I've decided to proceed with the "Ghost/Gray" state for the empty buckets. This involves updating `Bucket.tsx` to conditionally apply styles based on the `data.count`. When `count` is zero, I'll set a gray background, a dashed border, and a faint, grayscale icon, potentially with a "?" badge. However, drops are always allowed; the item must match the hidden type, a discovery. I will use a faint hint of the icon for educational value.


**Adjusting Chart Behavior**

I've decided to keep the chart visualization as-is. Seeing a bar "appear" from nothing aligns well with the bucket-filling concept. I will continue to focus on the gray visual cues to help kids understand the "emptiness" better. The chart can stay as a helpful and appropriate visual. I am moving on to other tasks that need to be addressed.


**Updating "Bucket.tsx"**

I'm now focusing on refining the `Bucket.tsx` component. I'm implementing a conditional style change based on the bucket's "emptiness." Empty buckets will display a "?" label and a faint outline icon, while filled buckets will show the full label and color icon. The goal is a clear visual distinction between empty and filled states. The associated logic in `App.tsx` requires no modification, as the visual updates are handled entirely within the `Bucket.tsx` component.


**Refining Visual Cues**

I'm now implementing the visual specification for `Bucket.tsx`. Empty buckets will have a gray background, a dashed gray border, a grayscale/outlined icon to hint at the shape, and the item name in gray as a label. The count will be hidden. In contrast, filled buckets will display the category color, a solid black border, the colored icon with a white background, and the label in white.


**Refining Visual Metaphor**

I'm now focusing on selling the "Empty Crate" metaphor in `Bucket.tsx`. I've decided on dashed lines and no fill, emphasizing the empty look. I've opted against dynamic slot creation due to complexity concerns. I'll also modify `LiveChart.tsx` to handle "unknown" categories more gracefully. The current labels are fine, acting as a scaffolding tool. I'm prioritizing minimal yet effective changes to the core functionality.


**Adjusting Label Visibility**

I'm currently working on the chart and considering how to represent empty categories. I've decided to update `LiveChart.tsx` so that the X-Axis labels are hidden for buckets with a count of zero. I can do this using a `tickFormatter` to provide dynamic control over label visibility. By updating the chart's X-axis, I think this will further emphasize the concept that categories are only "created" once items are sorted into them.

For at understøtte læringsmålet om at "kategorier opstår, når man sorterer", ændrer jeg designet således:

1.  **"Tomme" Spande:** Spandene starter som grå kasser med stiplede linjer (som tomme felter).
2.  **Visuel Feedback:**
    *   **Før sortering:** Man kan kun se et svagt omrids (skygge) af ikonet, så man kan ane formen, men farven og kategorinavnet er skjult. Der står blot "?" på kassen.
    *   **Efter sortering:** Så snart den første genstand lander i kassen, "opstår" kategorien: Kassen får sin farve, ikonet træder tydeligt frem, og navnet (f.eks. "ÆBLER") afsløres.
3.  **Grafen:** Grafen viser nu kun navnet på kategorien i bunden, når der faktisk er data i den.

Dette gør spillet mere udforskende og understreger pointen om, at en kategori defineres af dens indhold.

Her er ændringerne:

entries.description: Make buckets look like empty placeholders (dashed, gray, hidden label) until the first item is dropped.
Hide X-Axis labels for categories that have 0 items to reinforce the concept that the category doesn't exist yet.

payload.text: Jeg vil gerne have dem endnu mere usynlige. Således at man slet ikke kan se kategorien, men blot en "kasse" der er tom og grå

**Revising Initial Assumptions**

I've been re-evaluating the initial assumptions. The core objective is clear: achieving a higher degree of "invisibility" for the empty buckets, primarily concealing the category indication. The focus now is on how to make this concealment effective until the user interacts with the buckets.


**Prioritizing Visual Concealment**

I am now focusing on the visual changes needed in `Bucket.tsx`. My updated approach prioritizes completely hiding both the icon and the label on empty buckets. The goal is a uniform appearance: just an empty, gray box. The core challenge now is how to communicate drop targets effectively when all empty buckets are visually identical. I'm exploring placeholder solutions.


**Evaluating Drop Interactions**

My current thinking revolves around the user interaction with the now-empty buckets. Hiding the visuals is the easy part. The real complexity lies in the drop action and the resulting visual feedback. I'm considering three approaches: dynamic slot assignment, allowing drops on any empty bucket (requiring state changes), or maintaining pre-assigned buckets but visually obfuscating their labels. I'm leaning towards the third for its simplicity, despite its potential for user error, as the user did specify "simple".


**Refining Drop Mechanics**

I'm now fully immersed in the mechanics of the drop interaction. The previous ideas, dynamic assignment versus pre-defined slots with visual obfuscation, are refined. Given the goal of minimal code changes and user preference for a blank slate, I'm leaning toward pre-defined slots with hidden icons. However, a major concern arises: how will the user know where to drop items? I'm currently exploring the idea of a subtle visual hint on drag-over to communicate potential drop targets.


**Refining User Guidance**

I'm now fully immersed in the nuances of user guidance. Given the "more invisible" requirement, the core challenge is balancing concealment with usability. Should I allow complete invisibility until the drop, encouraging discovery, or offer subtle hints? My current leaning is towards pre-defined slots with no icon until drag-over. However, I am considering a very faint outline on a correct drop target upon drag-over for confirmation, while remaining completely hidden if incorrect, to satisfy the desire for "mere usynlige".


**Prioritizing Visual Concealment**

My current focus is on the `Bucket.tsx` file and the visual representation of empty buckets. I've removed the icon and label completely when a bucket is empty, leaving only the dashed box. The key now is the feedback users get as they drag. I'm building out logic that lets them learn by interacting with the boxes.

**Finalizing the Visual Representation**

The code is getting closer. With count 0, the icon and text are removed as per the spec, so there's now an empty, dashed box. The interaction feedback is the focus: If a drop is successful, then the bucket is updated. If the drop is rejected, that feedback also happens. The drag-and-drop mechanics seem complete.


**Rethinking Initial Constraints**

I'm now reconsidering the initial constraints. My priority is ensuring users can figure out the intended drop targets. I'm removing the help circle icon, the grayscale icon placeholder, and the labels as requested. The bucket is now just an empty box. I am investigating the possibility that the first drop in an empty bucket assigns the bucket's type.


**Optimizing Bucket Initialization**

I'm now fully immersed in the nuances of dynamic bucket creation. The goal is to allow users to establish categories via dropping objects into empty buckets. I'm exploring initializing buckets with a `null` type, or maintaining existing types but shuffling them. The "blind guessing" paradigm appears to satisfy the educational objectives best. I'm prioritizing the implementation of this "Create your own category" concept.


**Designing Advanced Swap Logic**

I am now focusing on implementing the "swap logic" within `App.tsx`. The core idea is to enable users to implicitly define bucket categories by dropping items into empty buckets. If I detect that an apple is dropped into a bucket that's currently empty but isn't already assigned to hold apples, I need to swap the underlying definitions of the bucket. This keeps existing definitions, but allows users to change definitions as well.


**Resolving Type Mismatches**

I'm now focusing on resolving the type mismatch during the drop event. The core issue is the `onDrop` event in `Bucket.tsx` only passes the item type, but I need to know the *target* bucket's type. I plan to change the `onDrop` function to pass the bucket index, which will allow me to track which bucket the user dropped onto. Then I can proceed to swap bucket configurations.


**Analyzing Drop Parameters**

I am now analyzing the `handleDrop` signature and logic to improve the interaction. I need to make sure the handleDrop function knows what was dragged and to which bucket. I'm removing the check within `Bucket.tsx` itself to allow the `App.tsx` parent to decide whether a drop is valid, and passing both the dragged item type and the target bucket's type to the parent handler. This enables me to implement the swapping logic effectively.


**Perfecting Swap Implementation**

My current focus is on the intricacies of the "swap" logic. The code refactoring is focused on the App.tsx implementation. The aim is for the desired drop and swap behavior, and creating a strong user experience is paramount. There is now a focus on improving the `App.tsx` file. The goal is to make sure users can create categories seamlessly by dropping items into empty buckets. The implementation also ensures the buckets will swap.


**Completing the Core Logic**

I'm now implementing the core swap logic within `App.tsx`. The `handleDrop` signature is updated to accept both the dropped item type and the target bucket type, which is exactly what I need. I'm removing the type check in `Bucket.tsx` to allow the parent component to manage the "wrong drop turned right" behavior. The focus is on ensuring users can create and modify categories seamlessly through item drops. I'm ensuring everything connects.


**Refining Bucket Swapping**

I am now focusing on the bucket swapping logic to be very intuitive. The update entails considering the scenario of dropping an item onto an empty, mis-assigned bucket. It looks like the simplest implementation is to swap the bucket's configuration with a different one. This is working now, and bucket state is successfully updated based on the type, label, icon, and colors.


**Finalizing Array Swapping**

I'm now implementing the array swapping logic for bucket re-assignment. If an incorrect item is dropped on an empty, pre-defined bucket, I'm swapping the bucket objects in the array. This correctly changes the bucket types visually for the user, making them suddenly the correct type. It's working seamlessly.


**Finalizing Item Validation**

I'm now integrating item validation with the state updates. The core issue is the need to conditionally update the `Items` state based on drop success. The approach I'm now taking involves validating the drop *before* the `setBuckets` update. By pre-calculating the validity, I can then control the item removal within the `setItems` state update, which allows me to provide feedback immediately. Now I am checking validity outside the setBuckets and items actions.

I have updated the logic to validate before. Also, I am taking into account the user's wish for "kasse der er tom og grå".

Det er en fantastisk pædagogisk pointe! Ved at lade den første genstand "definere" spanden, giver du børnene en langt mere intuitiv forståelse af sortering.

Her er ændringerne:

1.  **Helt usynlige spande:** I `Bucket.tsx` har jeg fjernet alt indhold (ikon, "?" og tekst), når spanden er tom. Den fremstår nu kun som en stiplet, grå ramme.
2.  **Dynamisk Sortering ("Magiske Spande"):** Jeg har opdateret logikken i `App.tsx`.
    *   Før var hver spand låst fast til en bestemt ting fra starten.
    *   Nu fungerer det sådan, at hvis du putter et æble i en *helt tom* kasse, så **bliver** den kasse til æble-kassen.
    *   Rent teknisk "bytter" jeg rundt på kategorierne i baggrunden, så spillet altid makker ret, uanset hvilken tom kasse barnet vælger at starte med.

Dette skaber følelsen af, at man selv "opretter" kategorien ved at placere den første ting.

entries.description: Completely hide icon and label when empty, and allow dropping any item on empty buckets to trigger creation logic
Implement dynamic category creation: dropping an item on an empty bucket reassigns that bucket to the dropped item's type.

payload.text:
Allersidste ting inden det er perfekt. Man skal kunne se diagrammet når man skal svare på sidste spørgsmål, som også gerne må varieres lidt (f.eks. flest og færrest)  SÅledes at det er aflæsningen som afgør svaret.

Slet også tippet i bunden. Det giver ikke mening

**Defining the Objective**

I'm currently dissecting the request. My primary objective is to perfect the final stage of this game. I've pinpointed that the bar chart's visibility during the quiz is critical for successful user interaction. I have Requirement 1 in focus, which is about the bar chart's visibility during the quiz.


**Refining the Quiz Logic**

I'm now focusing on requirement two and three, and the implications for coding. I'll need to expand question variety to include "fewest" and potentially others. The "Tip" section must be removed to align with the feedback. I'm considering layout changes in App.tsx to ensure the chart remains visible and readable during quiz mode, especially on various screen sizes.


**Optimizing Screen Real Estate**

I'm now zeroing in on screen real estate, particularly how the chart and quiz display across different devices. The side-by-side desktop layout works, but on mobile, stacking can easily push the chart out of view. I must prevent this, perhaps with a visual cue. I've also confirmed that the "Tip" section removal from the `App.tsx` file is crucial.

**Updating Prompt Instructions**

I have a plan to refine the prompt in `services/geminiService.ts`. I intend to sharpen it to specifically request questions around "flest" (most) and "færrest" (fewest) data reading from charts. The existing prompt is usable, but could be much more effective if it directly asks for chart-focused questions.


**Revising AI Prompting**

I'm making a strategic overhaul of the prompt within `geminiService.ts`. I have specified a new focus on questions that revolve around chart interpretation: finding the most, fewest, or specific quantities from the bar chart. I've also drafted the relevant code updates for both `App.tsx`, and `services/geminiService.ts`. I've removed the `mt-4 bg-yellow-50` block, and added Danish examples and a clear instruction to focus the AI on questions answerable by looking at the chart. Aesthetics have seen an improvement.

Det er modtaget. Her er de sidste justeringer for at gøre spillet helt skarpt:

1.  **Fokus på graflæsning i Quizzen:** Jeg har opdateret AI-instruktionerne (og fallback-logikken), så spørgsmålene nu fokuserer specifikt på at aflæse diagrammet. Der vil nu blive spurgt om både "Flest", "Færrest" og sammenligninger, så barnet er nødt til at kigge på grafen til højre.
2.  **Fjernet Tippet:** "Tip til børnene"-boksen i bunden er slettet, så designet er renere, og fokus holdes på søjlerne.

Her er ændringerne:

entries.description: Update prompt to focus specifically on chart reading (most/fewest) and add variety to fallback logic.
Removed the 'Tip' section from the bottom right to declutter the UI.

payload.text:
Tilføj så man kan vælge forskellige diagramtyper. Jeg vil gerne bede om et cirkeldiagram, et vandret søjediagram og et pindediagram (man skal kunne vælge mellem dem)

Derudover vil jeg gerne have knap så man kan vise/skjule tallet i toppen af søjlen, for at gøre aflæsningen mere svær.

**Defining the Core Elements**

I'm now zeroing in on the request, which is to implement selectable chart types (pie, bar, line/stick) and a value label toggle. I've broken down the project goals, focusing on the functionality to be added within the existing context.


**Analyzing the Requirements**

I'm now determining which existing files need the most attention. I'll need to define chart types in `types.ts`. The real work is in `LiveChart.tsx`, with significant logic updates for the different chart types and the value label toggle. I am maintaining the display preferences within the `LiveChart` component itself, to keep `App.tsx` clean, because it is the presentation layer.


**Refining the Chart Implementations**

I'm solidifying the chart type implementations, focusing on a "stick" chart modeled as a very thin Recharts bar. I'm considering using `ComposedChart` in the future for a scatter-bar combo, but a thin bar is suitable for now. I've also confirmed that showing/hiding labels will be a straightforward boolean toggle, conditionalizing the rendering of label properties in Recharts. This is shaping up nicely.


**Implementing the Component Logic**

I'm now integrating chart-type and label state into the UI. I will render different components based on the `chartType` state, and have created a simple toolbar at the top. I need to handle all the switch logic.

I am finalizing the chart type implementations. I have a plan for the horizontal bar, and the stick-chart will be a thin bar. I'll use `barSize=5` for the "stick" chart. The "hide label" toggle will be done via a conditional render of the label element in the component. The pie chart needs attention for `dataKey` and label placement.


**Coding the `LiveChart` Component**

I'm now fully immersed in coding the `LiveChart.tsx` details. I've imported the necessary Recharts components and the lucide-react icons for visual elements. I'm focusing on the render logic for each chart type. I'm handling the vertical and horizontal bar charts and implemented the pie and stick chart configurations. I'm also addressing the zero-value issue, as empty pie charts are visually unappealing. I'm ensuring the pie chart data is filtered to exclude zero-value entries to prevent clutter.


**Finalizing the Component Details**

I am now focusing on the final touches for `LiveChart.tsx`. I have reviewed the design choices and realized the chart component is self-contained and needs no update to `App.tsx`. I have included a state for the `activeChart` and `showValues`. The UI will include a toolbar with a dropdown or icons for the chart type, and a toggle for showing values. I am working on the final rendering for the `LiveChart.tsx` component, including the horizontal chart which will need the axes swapped, and adding a `Cell` map for colors. I will handle the "stick" diagram as a thin bar, which looks great. I'm making sure my code addresses the aesthetic constraints and language requirements.


**Updating the UI**

I've tackled the hide labels feature. For vertical and stick charts, I'm toggling the `<LabelList />`. Horizontal charts use `<LabelList />`, but the label positioning might need adjustment. For the pie chart, I'm conditionally rendering the `label` prop on the `<Pie />`. I am also working on `LiveChart.tsx`.

Her er ændringerne, der implementerer de ønskede funktioner:

1.  **Vælger til Diagramtype:** Der er tilføjet en række knapper i toppen af `LiveChart`, hvor man kan skifte mellem:
    *   **Søjlediagram (Lodret)** – Det klassiske.
    *   **Søjlediagram (Vandret)** – Godt til at se længdeforskelle.
    *   **Pindediagram** – Meget tynde søjler (ligner pinde/streger).
    *   **Cirkeldiagram (Lagkage)** – Viser fordelingen (kun synlig når der er data).

2.  **Vis/Skjul Tal:** En "øje"-knap gør det muligt at skjule tallene direkte på søjlerne/kakestykkerne. Dette tvinger eleven til at bruge akserne til at aflæse værdien, hvilket øger sværhedsgraden.

Her er koden:

entries.description: Add chart type selection (Vertical, Horizontal, Stick, Pie) and toggle for showing/hiding value labels.

diagnostics.message:
Type 'BucketData[]' is not assignable to type 'ChartDataInput[]'.

Index signature for type 'string' is missing in type 'BucketData'.

payload.text:

I'm now zeroing in on a TypeScript type mismatch within `LiveChart.tsx` at line 51. It seems `BucketData[]` is incompatible with `ChartDataInput[]`. I'm going to investigate what differences exist between `BucketData` and `ChartDataInput` to pinpoint the specific cause of this incompatibility and resolve the TypeScript error.


**Analyzing BucketData Issues**

I've determined the likely cause of the TypeScript error. The `BucketData` type used in `LiveChart.tsx` seems to be missing an index signature that Recharts expects, which is complaining about the type 'string'. I'll need to confirm if this is the case by examining how `dataKey` is used and then consider adding an index signature or reshaping the data to match Recharts' expectations more precisely.


**Pinpointing Data Incompatibility**

My thinking is leaning towards casting `activeData` directly when passed to the Recharts component. Casting seems preferable to modifying the type or model, if we can avoid it. Line 51 still seems to be the critical area to address.


**Considering Typecasting Solutions**

I'm now zeroing in on a simpler fix by typecasting the `activeData` prop to `any[]` directly within `LiveChart.tsx` for the PieChart. Considering the potential side effects of modifying the `BucketData` interface, I think this might be the safest short-term solution. I'm also anticipating that I will need to address a potentially similar issue with the `data` prop for the BarChart, so I will investigate that component of `LiveChart.tsx` next. This seems to be due to increased strictness in Recharts or @types/recharts.


**Implementing Typecasting Fix**

I'm now implementing the typecasting fix. My focus is on casting `activeData` to `any` within the `<Pie>` component in `LiveChart.tsx` to directly satisfy the TypeScript compiler. I'm taking a surgical approach, focusing on this specific component for now. I anticipate this should resolve the immediate error on line 51. The issue seems specific to Pie chart components.


**Applying Typecasting Fixes**

I am now proceeding with the planned typecasting within `LiveChart.tsx` to align with the stricter Recharts typing, focusing on the `data` prop of both `PieChart` and `BarChart` components. I've examined the code, confirming that I should cast both `activeData` and `data` to `any[]` where appropriate. Specifically, I intend to modify line 51 and line 76 to include the type assertion. This direct approach seems best, and I believe it satisfies the request.


**Refining Typecasting Strategy**

I've decided to refine the implementation. I'm modifying `LiveChart.tsx` to cast the `data` prop to `any[]` at the appropriate lines for all instances of `BarChart` and `PieChart`, resolving the type mismatch with Recharts. Specifically, I'm updating lines 51, 76, 118, and 149. Also, I'm now looking at GenAI usage since the prompt has added guidelines to follow.


**Evaluating Type Changes**

I'm now weighing the impact of altering `types.ts` versus the surgical casting strategy in `LiveChart.tsx`. Adding the index signature to `BucketData` seems globally safer than individual typecasts, but I need to consider the implications for the `icon` property. I'll determine if the index signature would affect how the icon is being handled. This could be the better long-term solution.


**Implementing Direct Typecast**

I'm now implementing the direct typecast as the solution. The focus is to address the TypeScript error, directly in `LiveChart.tsx`, with `any[]` for the data props. It seems safer for this application to avoid broader type alterations. My aim is to get a working implementation, so a focused change is best.


**Addressing PieChart Data Issues**

I'm now implementing the final touches. I'm focusing on the PieChart within `LiveChart.tsx`, ensuring `activeData` is correctly handled. I am proceeding by directly casting `activeData` to `any[]` and verifying the data mapping. I anticipate this will resolve the typing issues. I'll also ensure data integrity for other chart types.


**Verifying Data Integrity**

I'm confirming that typecasting to `any[]` and the mapping within the `<Pie>` component is properly resolving the typing errors. The chart should now render without TypeScript warnings. I've also verified that the remaining components in this part of the application can handle data of differing states.


**Confirming Code Modifications**

I'm now confirming the application of the typecasting changes and validating that the charts render correctly. I've re-reviewed lines 51, 76, 118, and 149 and verified that the `data` and `activeData` props are properly cast to `any[]` where Recharts expects it. I'm satisfied that these casts address the original TypeScript error.


**Refining BarChart Implementation**

I'm now zeroing in on the BarChart implementation within `LiveChart.tsx`. I'm reviewing the existing implementation and focusing on how the data is passed to the chart, particularly the 'count' dataKey and the handling of the 'fill' attribute.  "The width(-1) and height(-1) of chart should be greater than 0,

       or add a minWidth(0) or minHeight(undefined) or use aspect(undefined) to control the
       height and width." 

Jeg får disse to advarsler

**Addressing Recharts Warnings**

I'm currently investigating Recharts' warnings about negative width/height. It seems this stems from `ResponsiveContainer` trying to render before the parent container's dimensions are finalized, likely during the initial render or due to layout issues. I need to figure out how to ensure dimensions are available before the chart attempts rendering.


**Analyzing Parent Dimensions**

I've been focusing on dimension calculations and noticed `ResponsiveContainer`'s struggle with undefined parent sizes. The `LiveChart` component wraps the `ResponsiveContainer` within a `div` that includes `flex-grow min-h-[250px]`, but this isn't always enough to resolve warnings. I'm exploring the nuances of how the `height` and `min-height` settings interact with the `flex` layout in both `LiveChart.tsx` and `App.tsx` to get this working as needed.


**Investigating Layout Implications**

I'm now diving into the `LiveChart.tsx` component and the `App.tsx` layout. I've realized `ResponsiveContainer`'s issues with `flex-grow` parents are browser-dependent. A common fix involves explicit styles for the wrapper or a fallback aspect ratio, but ensuring explicit heights in the parent hierarchy might resolve things. I'm focusing on how `min-h-[250px]` in `LiveChart` and `h-[300px]`/`lg:h-auto` in `App.tsx` interact, particularly with `sticky` positioning.


**Clarifying Height Interactions**

I'm now focusing on how `h-full` in `LiveChart` interacts with `h-auto` and `min-h-[250px]` in its parent. Specifically, `h-full` within a container having `h-auto` can cause collapse. The Recharts warnings indicate failures in dimension measurement, possible in test environments or with hidden elements. Explicit height or `min-height` on the parent, or the `aspect` prop, remain the best solutions. I am examining explicit parent sizing and `position: relative`, which `ResponsiveContainer` should handle, to eliminate warnings.


**Pinpointing Height Conflicts**

I'm now investigating that `lg:h-auto` in `App.tsx` and `h-full` in `LiveChart.tsx` are likely the source of the trouble. If `App.tsx`'s container uses `h-auto`, the `LiveChart`'s `h-full` becomes ineffective, leading to flexbox issues. This is circular for `ResponsiveContainer` which relies on parent dimensions. Explicit sizes or `position: relative` for the container could solve these issues, also it is important the width of the container is set to 100%. I suspect initial render timing may also play a role.


**Refining Container Styling**

I'm now implementing `w-full` on the flex-grow container to guarantee width calculation. I'm also considering `min-width: 0` for flex children to address overflow, although that usually creates layout issues, not negative size warnings. The "width(-1) and height(-1)" warning suggests issues related to initial DOM state or hidden elements. I'll test changes in `App.tsx` and the `LiveChart` structure to ensure Recharts gets a definite size, also considering sticky positioning implications.



**Implementing Height Stabilization**

I'm now focusing on stabilizing height calculations, and am implementing width and height style attributes for the parent container of `ResponsiveContainer`. I will test changes in `App.tsx` and the wrapper divs inside `LiveChart` to provide a defined context. The height inconsistencies are likely from `lg:h-auto`, which interacts with the `min-h-[250px]` of the chart area. I am also considering giving the wrapper div in the `LiveChart` a specific class to guarantee space.


**Testing Absolute Positioning**

I'm now testing a new layout pattern within `LiveChart.tsx` to fix the Recharts warnings. I'm replacing the existing `div` with a relative parent and an absolute child that fills the parent using `inset-0`. With this approach, I need the parent div to have a defined height. I'm making sure `LiveChart`'s root is `h-full` and `App`'s container is explicitly sized. The absolute positioning approach should provide a definite size to the chart container, even with flexbox layout.


**Verifying Parent Sizing**

I'm now back in `LiveChart.tsx` to double-check the effects of various container styles. The key is defining clear height constraints for the `ResponsiveContainer`. I'm experimenting with absolute positioning within a `relative` parent, but I'll also try a simpler approach that incorporates `min-width: 0` on the parent to ensure correct flex layout behavior, in addition to `w-full` to ensure the container uses all available horizontal space. Then, I'll test the chart's rendering with and without `absolute inset-0` to confirm size stability.


**Applying Absolute Positioning**

I've implemented the absolute positioning pattern within `LiveChart.tsx` by wrapping the chart rendering within a `relative` container and an `absolute inset-0` child. The goal is now to ensure a definite size for the chart, even within the flex layout, and to eliminate the Recharts warning. The plan includes `w-full` for the root and `min-width: 0` for the flex item to resolve overflow issues, also ensuring that the wrapper of the rendered chart properly fills all available space. I've increased min-height to improve layout.


**Confirming Chart Size**

I'm now implementing the absolute positioning approach in `LiveChart.tsx` to ensure consistent dimension reporting to Recharts, even on initial render. I'm focusing on providing a definite size for the chart container, even with the flex layout in use. The wrapper `div` will use `relative` positioning, and the container for Recharts will use `absolute inset-0`, with the root container set to `w-full` and `min-width: 0`. This resolves the warnings, and should result in consistent charting.


**Refining Chart Rendering**

I'm now integrating the updated `LiveChart.tsx` component with the styling, ensuring consistent dimension calculations and stable rendering. I've focused on `w-full` for the flex parent and the `absolute inset-0` child inside a relative parent within the `LiveChart` component. This addresses Recharts warnings and fixes dimension issues, even with dynamic flex layout changes. My plan includes testing with both initial and dynamic data updates to confirm stability.



**Refining Pie Chart Integration**

I'm focusing on adapting the chart to the established sizing and positioning to ensure consistent display. I need to make sure the `PieChart` renders correctly within the `ResponsiveContainer`, addressing any remaining dimension-related issues, and to test interaction with different configurations.

Also, I'm examining how to apply different fills to the bars depending on their values. I've also worked on the bars using `dataKey`, `radius`, `animationDuration` and `barSize`, and implemented conditional fills for positive values, and testing that the label is correct.

The goal is to avoid the negative width warning by ensuring initial valid dimensions.


**Implementing Min-Dimensions**

Disse advarsler (warnings) opstår typisk, når `Recharts` prøver at tegne grafen, før containeren har fået en defineret størrelse fra browseren, hvilket resulterer i en bredde på 0 eller -1 under første "render".

Jeg har opdateret `LiveChart.tsx` ved at tilføje `minWidth={0}` til alle `ResponsiveContainer` komponenterne. Dette fortæller Recharts, at den skal acceptere en startbredde på 0 uden at brokke sig, indtil layoutet er helt på plads.

Her er den opdaterede kode:

entries.description: Add minWidth={0} to ResponsiveContainer to suppress Recharts resizing warnings.

payload.text: VI skal også have forskellen mellem størst og mindst ind i spørgsmålsdelen. Således at vi har minimumsværdi, maximumsværdi og variationsbredde. Men brug sprog der egner sig til indskoling. Måske også hvor mange der er i alt.

**Initiating Quiz Enhancements**

I'm currently focused on analyzing the request. The primary goal is to improve the quiz questions within the "Sorteringsfest" game, specifically for an "Indskoling" audience, roughly ages 6-7. I'm taking into account the need for age-appropriate language and concepts, with a focus on refining questions.


**Refining Quiz Logic**

I'm now diving into the specifics. I'm focusing on incorporating "Total" question types: "Hvor mange ting er der i alt?". I need to update the prompt sent to Gemini in `services/geminiService.ts` to include this. Additionally, I'm considering updating the fallback mechanism in case Gemini can't handle the request.


**Expanding Question Types**

I've expanded the range of quiz question types. Now, in addition to "Most", "Fewest", "Count Specific" and "Total," I'm incorporating the concept of "Difference" - e.g., "What's the difference between the most and least?". I'm also refining the fallback logic, ensuring a wider variety of questions when Gemini isn't able to answer, now incorporating a difference question. I'm focusing on calculating values like total items, max/min buckets, and the difference for fallback options, all in TypeScript.


**Defining Fallback Scenarios**

I'm developing concrete fallback scenarios. I'm focusing on "Most," "Fewest," "Total," and "Difference" question types. I'm generating answers and options for each of these cases to ensure robust quiz functionality even when Gemini fails. The specific question-answer pairs are now documented, so that the logic functions as intended.

**Updating Gemini Instructions**

I'm now refining the instructions given to Gemini. The goal is to explicitly list the allowable question types and ensure the response is in simple Danish. I'm focusing on clear directives within the prompt, specifically in the `services/geminiService.ts` file, to improve the accuracy and suitability of the quiz questions. I've updated the prompt to support "Difference" and "Total" questions.


**Implementing Fallback Strategies**

I'm now revising the fallback mechanism for question type selection. I plan to use a `switch` statement or a random index to choose among the five question types. This is to ensure variety and robustness in the event Gemini is unavailable. I'm also ensuring the total and difference questions will make sense when only a single type exists.

Det er en rigtig god idé at udvide spørgsmålene til også at omfatte variationsbredde og samlet antal (sum). Det styrker den matematiske forståelse.

Jeg opdaterer `services/geminiService.ts`.

Ændringerne omfatter:
1.  **Opdateret AI-prompt:** Jeg instruerer nu modellen i at kunne stille spørgsmål om "Total" og "Forskel" (variationsbredde) i et børnevenligt sprog.
2.  **Udvidet Fallback-logik:** Hvis AI'en fejler (eller der ikke er nogen API-nøgle), har jeg skrevet en ny logik, der tilfældigt vælger mellem 4 typer opgaver: *Flest*, *Færrest*, *I alt* og *Forskel*. Den genererer også dynamisk de forkerte svarmuligheder baseret på tallene.

Her er koden:

entries.description: Updated quiz generation to include questions about total sum and difference (range), with improved fallback logic.

payload.text: Du skal gøre så opgaverne formuleres lokalt, og ikke er afhængig af AI eller andre websites

**Isolating the Objective**

I'm currently focused on pinpointing the core goal: shifting quiz question generation to a local, browser-based process. I'm carefully analyzing the current implementation, and am working to decouple the dependency on external AI services.


**Refactoring the AI**

I'm now fully immersed in the refactoring effort. The plan involves surgically extracting the fallback logic from `geminiService.ts` and promoting it to be the primary question generation method. I've pinpointed the relevant files (`geminiService.ts`, `App.tsx`, and `index.html`), and I am ready to begin the code changes. I'll need to rewrite `geminiService.ts` to drop the AI dependency and to start using local logic.


**Implementing Local Quiz Logic**

I'm now implementing the core quiz generation logic. I've decided to rename `geminiService.ts` to `quizService.ts`, or leave it as is if renaming presents too much friction, and will remove the Gemini dependency from `index.html`. My local approach breaks down into input buckets, several calculations (active buckets, sorting, min/max values, totals, differences), and then various question types. I'm starting with implementing the question "Hvilken ting er der flest af?".


**Expanding Question Types**

I've expanded the question types, now including "Hvilken ting er der færrest af?", "Hvor mange ting er der i alt?", and "Hvad er forskellen på...?" (the last two only if counts differ). I'm also working on refining option generation; for text answers, I'll leverage other bucket labels, and for number answers, I'll generate numbers close to the correct answer. I am using a mental draft of the new `quizService.ts`, which removes all AI imports and has calculation logic. I'll use a `Set` to ensure unique options.


**Refining Local Logic**

I'm now refining the local logic for quiz generation. I'm focusing on handling only non-negative numbers in the questions. I've updated `index.html` to remove the `@google/genai` import and determined I'll modify `geminiService.ts` to include the local math, and also to update `App.tsx` to remove the loader since it won't be needed with the local generation. I'm also removing the await keyword.


**Prioritizing Code Simplicity**

I've decided to prioritize a more straightforward approach by updating the existing `services/geminiService.ts` with the local quiz generation code, instead of creating a new file. I'll focus on making this the path of least resistance to ensure I don't break the build due to missing files. I'll need to remove the GenAI dependency from `index.html`, and update `App.tsx` accordingly. I will keep the `loading_quiz` state but make the service fast; adding an artificial 500ms delay might be good for UX transitions.


**Finalizing Local Implementation**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the local quiz generation. I've updated `services/geminiService.ts` by removing the GoogleGenAI import and implementing the 4 question types, including a helper for generating non-negative, unique number options. I've also updated `index.html` to remove the import map for `@google/genai` and changed the loading text in `App.tsx` for clarity. I've confirmed that the core requirements are met.


**Confirming File Changes**

I'm now confirming the file changes are correct, particularly in `index.html`, removing the specified import map line. In `App.tsx`, I've updated the loading text for more generic clarity. I also need to be careful when modifying `index.html` to ensure the entire file is returned with the specified line removed. In `services/geminiService.ts`, I realized keeping the name is confusing but I will assume my refactoring handles this.


**Integrating Local Generation**

I'm now integrating the local quiz logic directly into `geminiService.ts`, effectively replacing the existing AI calls. This preserves file dependencies and prevents "Module not found" errors. I'm focusing on adapting the existing file to leverage local question generation functions, making sure the core app functionality continues as expected. I'm implementing the local number option generator to generate the options.


**Adapting the Implementation**

I'm now adapting the local logic. I'm focusing on the implementation of the random quiz question generation, with edge case handling, and UX consistency using a simulated delay. I've focused on generating the questions, then sorting the answer keys using `Math.random()`. The question types are also ready. I need to make sure that questions are generated regardless of the bucket data I get.


**Generating Question Types**

I'm now generating diverse quiz questions, incorporating logic for handling edge cases and ensuring consistent UX through a simulated delay. The questions and answer keys are ready, using the `Math.random()` function to randomize answer orders. I am now working on ensuring questions are generated no matter the bucket data. I'm focusing on ensuring all question types are implemented.


**Generating Diverse Questions**

I'm now generating diverse quiz questions by ensuring various types are present and function correctly. I've successfully implemented question generation for "most," "fewest," "total," and "difference" questions. I'm focusing on rigorous testing with diverse bucket data to identify any gaps in edge case handling. The answer key randomization using `Math.random()` is working seamlessly.

Jeg har omskrevet logikken, så spillet nu genererer alle matematikopgaverne lokalt direkte i browseren. Det betyder, at spillet ikke længere er afhængigt af en internetforbindelse til AI, og det kører hurtigere og mere stabilt.

Jeg har fjernet Google GenAI afhængigheden fra `index.html` og omskrevet `services/geminiService.ts` (jeg har beholdt filnavnet for at sikre, at appen stadig virker uden at skulle ændre alle import-stier, men indholdet er nu ren lokal matematik-logik).

Her er ændringerne:

entries.description: Removed @google/genai from importmap as logic is now local.
Replaced AI generation with local logic for generating math questions (Most, Fewest, Total, Difference).
Updated loading text to reflect local generation.
